title: Fitting a pattress back box in a solid masonry wall
subtitle: Cut a recess and install a metal back box in a brick or block wall
excerpt: How to mark out, cut, and chase a recess in a solid masonry wall for a standard 35 mm or 47 mm pattress back box, fit the box at the correct depth and level, and feed the cable into the box ready for the socket or switch faceplate.
sourceNotes: IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671:2018 + A2:2022). Collins Complete DIY Manual. BG Electrical and Click Scolmore back box installation notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A galvanised steel box that is set into the wall surface, providing the recess and mounting lugs for an electrical socket, switch, or data faceplate. Standard single sizes are 35 mm deep (for a single socket) and 47 mm deep (where cables or cable management require more space). Double-gang boxes are 86 mm wide × 86 mm high.

Cutting a narrow groove into a masonry or plaster surface to bury a cable or conduit. A chase must be covered with at least 6 mm of plaster or mortar, and must run in a zone — vertically above, beside, or horizontally from a switch or socket — so that future workers can locate the cable before drilling.

A pre-formed area of thin metal on the sides, back, or base of a back box that is removed (knocked out with a screwdriver) to create an aperture for the cable to enter the box. A grommet is fitted in the knockout hole to protect the cable sheath from the sharp metal edge.

content.text: into a solid masonry wall requires cutting a clean recess. The box must be exactly the right depth: flush with or fractionally below the plaster surface: so that the faceplate sits flat when fitted. Check that no pipes or cables are buried behind the chosen location before drilling. Use a cable and pipe detector before starting any drilling into a wall.
Step 1, check for buried services and mark the recess

Use a cable and pipe detector across the proposed back box position and the cable route. Mark the outline of the back box on the wall using the combination square and pencil: single gang 86 mm × 86 mm, double gang 147 mm × 86 mm. Mark the centre of the recess and the depth, equal to the box depth plus the plaster thickness above the masonry.

Score the perimeter of the box position with the SDS drill on chisel mode using a flat chisel bit. Then use the SDS chisel to remove the plaster and masonry within the marked area to the required depth. Work from the centre outward and check the depth frequently: the box must sit so that its face is flush with the finished wall surface. Vacuum or brush out loose material.
Cut a chase in the wall from the back box position to the cable origin, running vertically within the permitted zone for

. The chase need only be wide enough for the cable plus a conduit sleeve: 15 to 20 mm. Keep the chase depth consistent so the covering plaster is at least 6 mm. Feed the cable through the chase before fixing the box.

from the box side through which the cable will enter, and fit a rubber grommet in the hole. Offer the back box into the recess and mark through the two fixing holes onto the masonry. Drill masonry holes and fit wall plugs. Drive No. 8 × 25 mm screws through the back box into the plugs. Use the spirit level to ensure the box is perfectly level: a crooked back box means a crooked faceplate.
Step 5, make good the recess and chase

Fill around the box with plaster or a ready-mixed patching mortar, bringing the plaster flush with the wall face and the box face. Fill the cable chase to within the final coat thickness. Allow to cure, apply a finish coat, and sand smooth when dry.

On a hollow stud wall, a plastic dry-lining box (also called a plasterboard box) is used rather than a metal pattress. The dry-lining box has wings that fold out and grip the back of the plasterboard when a central screw is tightened. No drilling into the stud is required unless the box falls on a stud, in which case a metal pattress set into the stud is more secure.

attrs.symptom: Back box sits proud of the wall surface after fixing
attrs.cause: Recess not cut deep enough; box depth greater than the plaster depth at that location.
attrs.fix: Remove the box and deepen the recess in the masonry with the SDS chisel. Refit and check flush.

attrs.symptom: Box is not square to the wall — tilts away from the wall on one side
attrs.cause: Uneven recess bottom; wall plugs at different depths.
attrs.fix: Slacken both screws. Pack behind the low side with a small piece of scrap tile or hardboard until level, then retighten.

attrs.symptom: Cable sheath is damaged where it enters the back box
attrs.cause: Grommet not fitted in the knockout hole; sharp metal edge cutting into the sheath.
attrs.fix: Isolate the circuit. Fit a rubber grommet in all knockout holes the cable passes through. Inspect the cable sheath — if cut through the outer sheath only and the inner insulation is intact, re-route and fit the grommet; if the inner core is exposed, a new length of cable is required.
